If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
```json
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is the working MetroSense codebase: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer (✅ **exists and wired**, current status: skeleton with google_search tool only)\n- Treat the current implementation as the baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n- **Status**: ~70% complete. Auth flow, backend-to-agent routing, database schema, internal data routes, custom domain agent tools, structured response contract, and full subagent hierarchy are functional. Data seeding (script ready, not yet run), streaming, and E2E tests remain.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - The agents service runs behind a lightweight proxy that enforces the `X-Internal-Token` shared secret. The backend calls the proxy on port 8020, and the proxy forwards to the ADK server on port 8021. `/health` stays public for simple availability checks.\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8021 .`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run uvicorn app.proxy:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n\n### Implemented (✅)\n- **Backend:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me with JWT + httponly cookies), health checks, database schema (10+ models for weather/AQI/flooding/outages/traffic, sessions, conversation history), `/api/chat` endpoint with full `RiskCardPayload` and `ArtifactPayload` response schema\n- **Backend Internal Routes:** All `/internal/*` data routes (weather, AQI, lakes, floods, outages, traffic, ward profile, locations) — token-gated, consumed only by agent tools\n- **Backend Services:** `data_service.py` (all domain DB queries + neighbourhood→zone mapping), `conversation_service.py` (session upsert + turn append to `ConversationHistory`), `agent_proxy.py` (Level-2 and Level-1 ADK response parsing)\n- **Backend-to-Agent:** Proxy service with `X-Internal-Token` validation, session routing to ADK\n- **Agent Subagent Hierarchy:** Five-agent system (`root_agent → chat_agent → flood_vulnerability_agent / heat_health_agent / infrastructure_agent / logistics_agent`) using `gemini-2.5-flash`\n- **Agent Tools:** `flood_tools` (lake hydrology, flood incidents), `heat_tools` (AQI current/historical, ward profile), `infra_tools` (power outage events), `logistics_tools` (traffic current/corridor), `shared/weather_tools` (weather current/historical), `shared/location_tools` (resolve/list locations), `shared/document_tools` (list indexes, fetch section)\n- **Orchestration:** `routing.py` (keyword-based intent classification: flood/heat/infra/logistics/greeting), `response_contract.py` (`build_level2_response()` structured output builder)\n- **Prompts:** Domain-specific system prompts for all five agents; `CHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION` mandates JSON-only structured output with data freshness caveats and off-domain refusal\n- **Data Loader Script:** `server/scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py` — reads 6 CSVs and inserts reference + domain tables; run with `uv run python scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py --replace` (not yet executed)\n- **Frontend:** Chat UI (MessageBubble, MessageList, InputBar, ThinkingIndicator, SuggestedPrompts), auth context (login/signup/logout), RiskCard and ArtifactRenderer components, health polling\n- **Tests:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, health checks, basic chat endpoint\n\n### Planned (📋)\n- **Data Seeding:** `load_metrosense_dataset.py` exists and is ready to run; CSVs not yet loaded into PostgreSQL — run once before agent tools can return real data\n- **Document Ingestion:** Reference documents (`Documents_Metrosense/`) are read directly from the filesystem by `document_tools`; no DB ingestion needed\n- **Streaming:** Responses are buffered POST/response; SSE or WebSocket streaming not yet implemented\n- **Message Context Loading:** `ConversationHistory` model and `conversation_service` exist; conversation context not yet injected into agent session on new requests\n- **Structured Risk Card Generation:** End-to-end schema is wired (`RiskCardPayload` in routes, `build_level2_response()` in agents); requires data seeding + agent logic to populate fields\n- **E2E Tests:** Playwright configured; no test suites written yet\n- **Chat Response Tests:** Coverage for agent output parsing, risk card generation, artifact rendering\n\n### Current Test Coverage\n- Backend: Health, auth (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, basic chat routing\n- Frontend: Component renders (no E2E tests written yet)\n- Agents: smoke_test.py validates proxy routing only (not domain logic)\n\n### Next Steps (Recommended Order)\n1. **Run data loader** — `cd server && uv run python scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py --replace` to seed CSVs into PostgreSQL so agent tools return real data\n2. **Validate agent tool responses** — Smoke-test each domain tool end-to-end (internal routes → data_service → PostgreSQL) after seeding\n3. **Wire conversation context** — Pass recent `ConversationHistory` turns to the agent session so the agent has chat memory\n4. **Validate structured output** — Confirm `build_level2_response()` + `RiskCardPayload` round-trip produces renderable frontend cards\n5. **Implement streaming** — Add SSE or WebSocket to `/api/chat` for incremental text delivery\n6. **Write E2E tests** — Playwright suites covering login → chat → risk card render\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- **Important:** The CSV data files (`DataSet_MetroSense/`) exist in the repo and the loader script (`server/scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py`) is implemented but **not yet run**. Seed the database before expecting agent domain tools to return real data.\n- Documents (`Documents_Metrosense/`) are served directly from the filesystem by `document_tools` — no DB load needed, but `DOCUMENTS_PATH` env must point to the correct directory.\n- The `metrosense_agent/` is the production agent; `metrosearch_agent/` is a legacy skeleton that can be removed. All five subagents and their domain tools are implemented and wired into the ADK hierarchy.\n- The `response_contract.py` `build_level2_response()` builder and all Pydantic schemas (`RiskCardPayload`, `ArtifactPayload`) are in place end-to-end — the remaining gap is data availability and agent logic populating those fields.\n- If current behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- google-adk-python: Expert guidance on the Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) for Python. Use this skill when the user asks about building agents, using tools, streaming, callbacks, tutorials, deployment, or advanced architecture with the Google ADK in Python. (file: /home/dell/ai-track-metrosense/.agents/skills/google-adk-python/SKILL.md)\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
```
```json
    }
```
```json
  }
}
```

dataclass\nimport re\n\nFLOOD_KEYWORDS = {\n    \"flood\",\n    \"rain\",\n    \"inundation\",\n    \"drainage\",\n    \"lake\",\n    \"waterlogging\",\n    \"barricade\",\n}\nHEAT_KEYWORDS = {\n    \"aqi\",\n    \"air quality\",\n    \"heat\",\n    \"uhi\",\n    \"pollution\",\n    \"pm2.5\",\n    \"pm10\",\n    \"no2\",\n}\nINFRA_KEYWORDS = {\n    \"power\",\n    \"outage\",\n    \"grid\",\n    \"bescom\",\n    \"tree fall\",\n    \"wind\",\n}\nLOGISTICS_KEYWORDS = {\n    \"traffic\",\n    \"corridor\",\n    \"delay\",\n    \"route\",\n    \"logistics\",\n    \"orr\",\n    \"hosur\",\n}\nSCORECARD_KEYWORDS = {\"risk\", \"scorecard\", \"vulnerability\", \"assessment\", \"how vulnerable is\"}\nGREETING_PHRASES = {\n    \"hi\",\n    \"hello\",\n    \"hey\",\n    \"good morning\",\n    \"good afternoon\",\n    \"good evening\",\n    \"namaste\",\n}\nGREETING_ALLOWED_TOKENS = {\n    \"hi\",\n    \"hello\",\n    \"hey\",\n    \"good\",\n    \"morning\",\n    \"afternoon\",\n    \"evening\",\n    \"namaste\",\n    \"there\",\n    \"team\",\n    \"metrosense\",\n    \"metro\",\n    \"sense\",\n    \"agent\",\n}\n\n\n@dataclass(frozen=True)\nclass IntentClassification:\n    flood: bool\n    heat: bool\n    infra: bool\n    logistics: bool\n\n\ndef _matches_any(text: str, keywords: set[str]) -> bool:\n    return any(keyword in text for keyword in keywords)\n\n\ndef classify_intents(message: str) -> IntentClassification:\n    text = message.lower()\n    return IntentClassification(\n        flood=_matches_any(text, FLOOD_KEYWORDS),\n        heat=_matches_any(text, HEAT_KEYWORDS),\n        infra=_matches_any(text, INFRA_KEYWORDS),\n        logistics=_matches_any(text, LOGISTICS_KEYWORDS),\n    )\n\n\ndef should_trigger_scorecard(message: str) -> bool:\n    return _matches_any(message.lower(), SCORECARD_KEYWORDS)\n\n\ndef determine_response_mode(message: str) -> str:\n    if should_trigger_scorecard(message):\n        return \"scorecard\"\n    return \"text\"\n\n\ndef _normalize_text(message: str) -> str:\n    lowered = message.lower().strip()\n    cleaned = re.sub(r\"[^a-z0-9\\s]\", \" \", lowered)\n    return \" \".join(cleaned.split())\n\n\ndef is_greeting_only(message: str) -> bool:\n    normalized = _normalize_text(message)\n    if not normalized:\n        return False\n\n    if normalized in GREETING_PHRASES:\n        return True\n\n    # If query intent words exist, this is not greeting-only.\n    if _matches_any(normalized, FLOOD_KEYWORDS | HEAT_KEYWORDS | INFRA_KEYWORDS | LOGISTICS_KEYWORDS):\n        return False\n    if _matches_any(normalized, SCORECARD_KEYWORDS):\n        return False\n\n    tokens = set(normalized.split())\n    contains_greeting_token = any(token in GREETING_ALLOWED_TOKENS for token in tokens) and (\n        \"hi\" in tokens\n        or \"hello\" in tokens\n        or \"hey\" in tokens\n        or \"namaste\" in tokens\n        or (\"good\" in tokens and (\"morning\" in tokens or \"afternoon\" in tokens or \"evening\" in tokens))\n    )\n    return contains_greeting_token and tokens.issubset(GREETING_ALLOWED_TOKENS)\n"
```json
  }
}
```

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:45:19.608Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "I’ve confirmed the current shape: `chat_agent` is the only user-facing orchestrator, there is no comparison-specific agent yet, and the existing domain tools already expose summary/historical endpoints that can support comparison requests. I’m doing one more inspection pass on the backend summary payloads so the plan can be concrete about what gets normalized into table artifacts and where.",
    "phase": "commentary"
  }
}
```

## Entry 530
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T18:45:19.668Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:45:19.668Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_iLtxpX98D3WsiRjeCELTIoYu",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 75159a\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 2856\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom datetime import UTC, datetime, timedelta\nfrom typing import Any\n\nfrom sqlalchemy import String, cast, func, or_, select\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession\nfrom sqlalchemy.inspection import inspect\n\nfrom app.db.models import (\n    AirQualityObservation,\n    FloodIncident,\n    LakeHydrology,\n    LocationMaster,\n    PowerOutageEvent,\n    TrafficSegmentObservation,\n    WardProfile,\n    WeatherObservation,\n)\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Neighbourhood → zone mapping for weather and traffic lookups.\n# Weather and traffic data are stored per zone (zone_north/east/south/west/cbd)\n# while other domains use plain neighbourhood names or ward_ids.\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n_NEIGHBORHOOD_ZONE_MAP: dict[str, str] = {\n    \"Hebbal\": \"zone_north\",\n    \"Manyata Tech Park\": \"zone_north\",\n    \"Yelahanka\": \"zone_north\",\n    \"KR Puram\": \"zone_east\",\n    \"Whitefield\": \"zone_east\",\n    \"Varthur\": \"zone_east\",\n    \"Bellandur\": \"zone_east\",\n    \"Koramangala\": \"zone_south\",\n    \"BTM Layout\": \"zone_south\",\n    \"Jayanagar\": \"zone_south\",\n    \"Silk Board\": \"zone_south\",\n    \"Sarjapur\": \"zone_south\",\n    \"Peenya\": \"zone_west\",\n    \"Rajajinagar\": \"zone_west\",\n    \"Yeshwanthpur\": \"zone_west\",\n    \"MG Road\": \"zone_cbd\",\n    \"Indiranagar\": \"zone_cbd\",\n}\n\n\ndef _row_to_dict(row: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    mapper = inspect(type(row))\n    payload: dict[str, Any] = {}\n    for column in mapper.columns:\n        payload[column.key] = getattr(row, column.key)\n    return payload\n\n\nasync def _resolve_zone_id(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Resolve a neighbourhood name or ward_id to the zone_id used in weather/traffic tables.\n\n    Falls back to the original value if no mapping can be found so that\n    callers passing a zone_id directly still work.\n    \"\"\"\n    if location_id.startswith(\"zone_\"):\n        return location_id\n    # Fast static lookup by canonical name\n    if location_id in _NEIGHBORHOOD_ZONE_MAP:\n        return _NEIGHBORHOOD_ZONE_MAP[location_id]\n    # Try location_master zone_name (covers entries seeded with a zone)\n    stmt = (\n        select(LocationMaster.zone_name)\n        .where(\n            or_(\n                LocationMaster.location_id == location_id,\n                LocationMaster.canonical_name == location_id,\n            ),\n            LocationMaster.zone_name.is_not(None),\n        )\n        .limit(1)\n    )\n    zone: str | None = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalar()\n    if zone:\n        return zone\n    # Fall back: try to match the canonical_name against the static map\n    name_stmt = (\n        select(LocationMaster.canonical_name)\n        .where(LocationMaster.location_id == location_id)\n        .limit(1)\n    )\n    canonical: str | None = (await session.execute(name_stmt)).scalar()\n    return _NEIGHBORHOOD_ZONE_MAP.get(canonical or \"\", location_id)\n\n\nasync def resolve_zone_id(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> str:\n    return await _resolve_zone_id(session, location_id)\n\n\nasync def _resolve_ward_id(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Resolve a neighbourhood name or plain location_id to its ward_id.\n\n    Flood and outage data are stored using ward_ids (e.g. ward_007).\n    \"\"\"\n    if location_id.startswith(\"ward_\"):\n        return location_id\n    stmt = (\n        select(LocationMaster.location_id)\n        .where(\n            or_(\n                LocationMaster.location_id == location_id,\n                LocationMaster.canonical_name == location_id,\n            ),\n            LocationMaster.location_type == \"ward\",\n        )\n        .limit(1)\n    )\n    ward: str | None = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalar()\n    return ward if ward else location_id\n\n\nasync def resolve_ward_id(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> str:\n    return await _resolve_ward_id(session, location_id)\n\n\nasync def _dataset_anchor(session: AsyncSession, ts_col: Any) -> datetime:\n    \"\"\"Return MAX(ts_col) as the reference anchor for historical window queries.\n\n    Using the dataset's own latest timestamp instead of datetime.now() means\n    static / historical datasets remain queryable regardless of when the server runs.\n    \"\"\"\n    result = await session.execute(select(func.max(ts_col)))\n    anchor: datetime | None = result.scalar()\n    return anchor if anchor is not None else datetime.now(UTC)\n\n\nasync def get_weather_current(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, limit: int = 1\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    zone_id = await _resolve_zone_id(session, location_id)\n    stmt = (\n        select(WeatherObservation)\n        .where(WeatherObservation.location_id == zone_id)\n        .order_by(WeatherObservation.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_weather_historical(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, hours: int = 720\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    zone_id = await _resolve_zone_id(session, location_id)\n    anchor = await _dataset_anchor(session, WeatherObservation.observed_at)\n    window_start = anchor - timedelta(hours=hours)\n    stmt = (\n        select(WeatherObservation)\n        .where(\n            WeatherObservation.location_id == zone_id,\n            WeatherObservation.observed_at >= window_start,\n        )\n        .order_by(WeatherObservation.observed_at.desc())\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_aqi_current(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, limit: int = 1\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    stmt = (\n        select(AirQualityObservation)\n        .where(AirQualityObservation.location_id == location_id)\n        .order_by(AirQualityObservation.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_aqi_historical(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, days: int = 365\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    anchor = await _dataset_anchor(session, AirQualityObservation.observed_at)\n    window_start = anchor - timedelta(days=days)\n    stmt = (\n        select(AirQualityObservation)\n        .where(\n            AirQualityObservation.location_id == location_id,\n            AirQualityObservation.observed_at >= window_start,\n        )\n        .order_by(AirQualityObservation.observed_at.desc())\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_lake_hydrology(\n    session: AsyncSession, lake_id: str, limit: int = 5\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    stmt = (\n        select(LakeHydrology)\n        .where(LakeHydrology.lake_id == lake_id)\n        .order_by(LakeHydrology.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_flood_incidents(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, limit: int = 20\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    ward_id = await _resolve_ward_id(session, location_id)\n    stmt = (\n        select(FloodIncident)\n        .where(FloodIncident.location_id == ward_id)\n        .order_by(FloodIncident.reported_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_power_outages(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, window_days: int = 365\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    ward_id = await _resolve_ward_id(session, location_id)\n    anchor = await _dataset_anchor(session, PowerOutageEvent.started_at)\n    window_start = anchor - timedelta(days=window_days)\n    stmt = (\n        select(PowerOutageEvent)\n        .where(\n            PowerOutageEvent.location_id == ward_id,\n            PowerOutageEvent.started_at >= window_start,\n            or_(\n                PowerOutageEvent.outage_type.is_(None),\n                func.lower(PowerOutageEvent.outage_type) != \"planned\",\n            ),\n        )\n        .order_by(PowerOutageEvent.started_at.desc())\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_traffic_current(\n    session: AsyncSession, location_id: str, limit: int = 5\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    zone_id = await _resolve_zone_id(session, location_id)\n    stmt = (\n        select(TrafficSegmentObservation)\n        .where(TrafficSegmentObservation.location_id == zone_id)\n        .order_by(TrafficSegmentObservation.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_traffic_corridor(\n    session: AsyncSession, corridor_name: str, limit: int = 20\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    stmt = (\n        select(TrafficSegmentObservation)\n        .where(func.lower(TrafficSegmentObservation.corridor_name) == corridor_name.lower())\n        .order_by(TrafficSegmentObservation.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def resolve_location(session: AsyncSession, name: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    pattern = f\"%{name}%\"\n    stmt = (\n        select(LocationMaster)\n        .where(\n            or_(\n                LocationMaster.canonical_name.ilike(pattern),\n                cast(LocationMaster.aliases, String).ilike(pattern),\n            )\n        )\n        .order_by(LocationMaster.canonical_name.asc())\n        .limit(20)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def list_locations(session: AsyncSession, limit: int = 200) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    stmt = select(LocationMaster).order_by(LocationMaster.canonical_name.asc()).limit(limit)\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_ward_profile(session: AsyncSession, ward_id: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None:\n    stmt = select(WardProfile).where(WardProfile.ward_id == ward_id).limit(1)\n    row = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().first()\n    if row is None:\n        return None\n    return _row_to_dict(row)\n\n\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n# Aggregation queries — return compact monthly/ranked summaries instead of\n# raw time-series rows so the agent can reason over the data directly.\n# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n\nasync def get_aqi_summary(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"Monthly AQI aggregates for a neighbourhood — up to 12 rows for a full year.\n\n    Returns avg/min/max AQI and dominant category per calendar month.\n    Use this instead of get_aqi_historical for annual trends or year-long summaries.\n    \"\"\"\n    # Step 1: monthly aggregate stats\n    aqi_month = func.date_trunc(\"month\", AirQualityObservation.observed_at)\n    agg_stmt = (\n        select(\n            aqi_month.label(\"month\"),\n            func.avg(AirQualityObservation.aqi_value).label(\"avg_aqi\"),\n            func.min(AirQualityObservation.aqi_value).label(\"min_aqi\"),\n            func.max(AirQualityObservation.aqi_value).label(\"max_aqi\"),\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 533
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T18:45:22.891Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:45:22.891Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_wbecrX6h0a0gJWrds6Kdfja1",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 854f24\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1285\nOutput:\n            func.max(AirQualityObservation.aqi_value).label(\"max_aqi\"),\n            func.count().label(\"readings\"),\n        )\n        .where(AirQualityObservation.location_id == location_id)\n        .group_by(aqi_month)\n        .order_by(aqi_month)\n    )\n    agg_rows = (await session.execute(agg_stmt)).all()\n\n    # Step 2: category counts per month — pick dominant in Python\n    # Reuse the same expression object so asyncpg prepared statements share one bind param\n    cat_month = func.date_trunc(\"month\", AirQualityObservation.observed_at)\n    cat_stmt = (\n        select(\n            cat_month.label(\"month\"),\n            AirQualityObservation.aqi_category,\n            func.count().label(\"cnt\"),\n        )\n        .where(\n            AirQualityObservation.location_id == location_id,\n            AirQualityObservation.aqi_category.is_not(None),\n        )\n        .group_by(\n            cat_month,\n            AirQualityObservation.aqi_category,\n        )\n        .order_by(\n            cat_month,\n            func.count().desc(),\n        )\n    )\n    cat_rows = (await session.execute(cat_stmt)).all()\n\n    # Build month → dominant category map (first row per month has highest count)\n    dom_map: dict[str, str] = {}\n    for r in cat_rows:\n        key = str(r.month)[:7]\n        if key not in dom_map and r.aqi_category:\n            dom_map[key] = r.aqi_category\n\n    return [\n        {\n            \"month\": str(r.month)[:7],\n            \"location_id\": location_id,\n            \"avg_aqi\": round(float(r.avg_aqi)) if r.avg_aqi is not None else None,\n            \"min_aqi\": r.min_aqi,\n            \"max_aqi\": r.max_aqi,\n            \"dominant_category\": dom_map.get(str(r.month)[:7]),\n            \"readings\": r.readings,\n        }\n        for r in agg_rows\n    ]\n\n\nasync def get_weather_summary(session: AsyncSession, location_id: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"Monthly weather aggregates for a zone — up to 12 rows for a full year.\n\n    Returns avg/max/min temperature, avg humidity, and total rainfall per month.\n    Use this instead of get_weather_historical for annual or seasonal analysis.\n    \"\"\"\n    zone_id = await _resolve_zone_id(session, location_id)\n    month_expr = func.date_trunc(\"month\", WeatherObservation.observed_at)\n    month_col = month_expr.label(\"month\")\n    stmt = (\n        select(\n            month_col,\n            func.avg(WeatherObservation.temperature_celsius).label(\"avg_temp_c\"),\n            func.max(WeatherObservation.temperature_celsius).label(\"max_temp_c\"),\n            func.min(WeatherObservation.temperature_celsius).label(\"min_temp_c\"),\n            func.avg(WeatherObservation.humidity_percent).label(\"avg_humidity_pct\"),\n            func.sum(WeatherObservation.rainfall_mm_hourly).label(\"total_rainfall_mm\"),\n            func.count().label(\"readings\"),\n        )\n        .where(WeatherObservation.location_id == zone_id)\n        .group_by(month_expr)\n        .order_by(month_expr)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).all()\n\n    def _r1(v: Any) -> float | None:\n        return round(float(v), 1) if v is not None else None\n\n    return [\n        {\n            \"month\": str(r.month)[:7],\n            \"zone_id\": zone_id,\n            \"avg_temp_c\": _r1(r.avg_temp_c),\n            \"max_temp_c\": _r1(r.max_temp_c),\n            \"min_temp_c\": _r1(r.min_temp_c),\n            \"avg_humidity_pct\": _r1(r.avg_humidity_pct),\n            \"total_rainfall_mm\": _r1(r.total_rainfall_mm),\n            \"readings\": r.readings,\n        }\n        for r in rows\n    ]\n\n\nasync def get_weather_extremes(\n    session: AsyncSession,\n    metric: str = \"temperature_celsius\",\n    top_n: int = 10,\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"Top N extreme days across all zones ranked by the chosen metric.\n\n    metric: 'temperature_celsius' (hottest days) or 'rainfall_mm_24hour' (wettest days).\n    Returns date, zone_id, station_name, and the metric value.\n    Use this to answer 'hottest day in 2023' or 'which area had the most rain'.\n    \"\"\"\n    allowed_metrics = {\"temperature_celsius\", \"rainfall_mm_24hour\"}\n    if metric not in allowed_metrics:\n        metric = \"temperature_celsius\"\n\n    obs_col = (\n        WeatherObservation.temperature_celsius\n        if metric == \"temperature_celsius\"\n        else WeatherObservation.rainfall_mm_24hour\n    )\n\n    date_expr = func.date_trunc(\"day\", WeatherObservation.observed_at)\n    date_col = date_expr.label(\"date\")\n    agg_val = func.max(obs_col).label(\"value\")\n\n    stmt = (\n        select(\n            date_col,\n            WeatherObservation.location_id,\n            WeatherObservation.station_name,\n            agg_val,\n        )\n        .where(obs_col.is_not(None))\n        .group_by(date_expr, WeatherObservation.location_id, WeatherObservation.station_name)\n        .order_by(agg_val.desc())\n        .limit(top_n)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).all()\n    return [\n        {\n            \"date\": str(r.date)[:10],\n            \"zone_id\": r.location_id,\n            \"station_name\": r.station_name,\n            \"metric\": metric,\n            \"value\": round(float(r.value), 1) if r.value is not None else None,\n        }\n        for r in rows\n    ]\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 535
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T18:45:27.082Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:45:27.082Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "The current repo can support comparison cleanly without new public APIs: the domain summary endpoints already give monthly aggregates for weather and AQI, and the chat stack already supports a canonical `table` artifact. The main product choices left are scope and architecture, because those materially change the plan.",
    "phase": "commentary"
  }
}
```

```json
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "request_user_input",
    "arguments": "{\"questions\":[{\"header\":\"Scope\",\"id\":\"comparison_scope\",\"question\":\"What scope do you want for the first comparison capability?\", \"options\":[{\"label\":\"Same-domain first (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Support arbitrary comparisons within one domain at a time, like weather month-vs-month or AQI location-vs-location, using existing summary/historical tools.\"},{\"label\":\"Cross-domain too\",\"description\":\"Also support mixed comparisons like rainfall vs AQI in one table, which requires a broader normalization layer and more ambiguity handling.\"},{\"label\":\"Weather only first\",\"description\":\"Ship a narrow but reliable weather-comparison flow first, then expand later.\"}]},{\"header\":\"Architecture\",\"id\":\"comparison_arch\",\"question\":\"Which implementation style do you want for v1?\", \"options\":[{\"label\":\"Comparison agent + builder (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Add an internal comparison subagent for intent/tool orchestration, backed by deterministic Python table-building utilities.\"},{\"label\":\"Builder only\",\"description\":\"Keep one chat agent and do comparison routing/building mostly in deterministic Python without adding another subagent.\"},{\"label\":\"Prompt-only\",\"description\":\"Rely on the existing chat agent prompt to produce comparison tables directly, with minimal new code.\"}]}]}",
    "call_id": "call_X9VOTCjUqmqJalqmmplE1mkU"
  }
}
```

## Entry 541
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T18:48:26.430Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:48:26.430Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "item_completed",
    "thread_id": "019cde1d-7859-7f53-a082-695df578ad89",
    "turn_id": "019cde37-730d-7523-b58a-ff17f6abe0c8",
    "item": {
      "type": "Plan",
      "id": "019cde37-730d-7523-b58a-ff17f6abe0c8-plan",
      "text": "# Comparison Capability End-to-End\n\n## Summary\nBuild a first-class comparison flow inside the existing `metrosense_agent` stack for same-domain comparisons, with a dedicated internal `comparison_agent` backed by deterministic Python table-building utilities.\n\nV1 scope:\n- Support arbitrary comparisons within a single domain at a time\n- Primary initial domains: weather and AQI, because summary endpoints already exist and fit tabular comparisons well\n- Output must always be canonical MetroSense chat payloads: concise `response_text` plus `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n\nChosen defaults:\n- Architecture: `comparison_agent` for intent/tool orchestration + deterministic backend/agent-side comparison builder for table construction\n- No new public browser-facing API\n- No prompt-only comparison generation except as a fallback; table assembly should be code-driven\n\n## Key Changes\n### Agent orchestration\n- Add an internal `comparison_agent` under `chat_agent`.\n- Extend intent routing to explicitly detect comparison/table requests:\n  - `compare`, `comparison`, `vs`, `versus`, `table`, `month over month`, `year over year`, `location A vs location B`\n- Route only same-domain comparisons to `comparison_agent` in v1.\n- Keep scorecards and predictive reasoning on existing paths.\n\n### Comparison contract\n- Define a comparison intermediate model in Python, not prompt text:\n  - resolved domain\n  - comparison axis: time, location, or metric subset\n  - entities/periods to compare\n  - canonical table title\n  - normalized rows/columns\n  - summary sentence(s)\n  - freshness/citation metadata\n- `comparison_agent` should decide what to compare and which domain tools to call, but Python should build the final table artifact.\n\n### Deterministic comparison builder\n- Add a shared comparison builder utility in the agents layer or orchestration layer.\n- Builder responsibilities:\n  - accept tool outputs from one domain only\n  - align records by comparison axis\n  - choose stable column headers\n  - map raw keys to user-facing metric labels\n  - emit canonical `artifact.type = \"table\"` shape\n  - generate a compact prose summary and freshness note\n- Builder should support these v1 patterns:\n  - time comparison within one location\n  - location comparison within one period\n  - month/year breakdown comparisons from summary endpoints\n- If the requested comparison cannot be formed from existing data, return a clean refusal/limitation in `response_text` with no malformed artifact.\n\n### Tool usage by domain\n- Weather:\n  - prefer `get_weather_summary` for month/year/season comparisons\n  - fall back to `get_weather_historical` only when summary data cannot answer the request\n- AQI:\n  - prefer `get_aqi_summary` for month/year comparisons\n  - use `get_aqi_historical` for short-window comparisons\n- Do not add new backend endpoints for v1 unless an uncovered same-domain comparison is impossible with current tools.\n- Do not support mixed-domain tables in v1.\n\n### Prompt updates\n- Update `chat_agent` prompt to hand comparison/table requests to `comparison_agent`.\n- Add a dedicated `comparison_agent` prompt that focuses on:\n  - extracting domain, entities, periods, and requested metrics\n  - calling only relevant domain tools\n  - returning structured comparison inputs, not free-form `summary/details` blobs\n- Remove reliance on legacy prompt patterns that emit raw dicts.\n\n### Backend compatibility\n- Keep the current backend parser compatibility layer for old malformed outputs during transition.\n- Treat the new `comparison_agent` output format as canonical and preferred.\n- Do not expand legacy-shape parsing further once the new comparison path is live unless a concrete regression is found.\n\n### Frontend behavior\n- Keep the existing `table` artifact renderer as the only rendering path for comparison tables.\n- Ensure refreshed/restored chats continue to render saved comparison responses correctly in live sessions.\n- If history replay later includes structured artifacts, reuse the same `table` artifact contract.\n\n## Interfaces / Public Types\n- Keep the public `/api/chat` response contract stable.\n- Canonical comparison response:\n  - `response_text: string`\n  - `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n  - `artifact.title: string`\n  - `artifact.columns: list[string]`\n  - `artifact.rows: list[list[string | number | null]]`\n  - optional `artifact.description`\n- Add internal comparison-agent structured output contract for orchestration only; it should not be exposed to the browser.\n\n## Test Plan\n- Agent/orchestration tests:\n  - comparison intent detection for `compare`, `vs`, `table`\n  - same-domain routing goes to `comparison_agent`\n  - non-comparison historical queries still route to existing domain agents\n- Comparison-builder tests:\n  - weather month-vs-month comparison in one location\n  - AQI location-vs-location comparison for one period\n  - missing month or sparse data handling\n  - unsupported mixed-domain request returns a limitation, not malformed output\n- Backend/API tests:\n  - `/api/chat` accepts canonical `table` artifacts from the new comparison flow\n  - legacy compatibility still works during transition\n- Frontend tests:\n  - `table` artifact renders with correct headers/rows\n  - restored active session still displays comparison tables after refresh\n\n## Assumptions\n- V1 comparison scope is same-domain only.\n- Weather and AQI are the first-class comparison domains in v1.\n- Existing summary/historical tools are sufficient for the first iteration.\n- Comparison table generation should be deterministic in code, with the LLM used for intent extraction and tool orchestration rather than free-form table authoring.\n"
    }
  }
}
```
**Metadata:**

```json
      "text": "# Comparison Capability End-to-End\n\n## Summary\nBuild a first-class comparison flow inside the existing `metrosense_agent` stack for same-domain comparisons, with a dedicated internal `comparison_agent` backed by deterministic Python table-building utilities.\n\nV1 scope:\n- Support arbitrary comparisons within a single domain at a time\n- Primary initial domains: weather and AQI, because summary endpoints already exist and fit tabular comparisons well\n- Output must always be canonical MetroSense chat payloads: concise `response_text` plus `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n\nChosen defaults:\n- Architecture: `comparison_agent` for intent/tool orchestration + deterministic backend/agent-side comparison builder for table construction\n- No new public browser-facing API\n- No prompt-only comparison generation except as a fallback; table assembly should be code-driven\n\n## Key Changes\n### Agent orchestration\n- Add an internal `comparison_agent` under `chat_agent`.\n- Extend intent routing to explicitly detect comparison/table requests:\n  - `compare`, `comparison`, `vs`, `versus`, `table`, `month over month`, `year over year`, `location A vs location B`\n- Route only same-domain comparisons to `comparison_agent` in v1.\n- Keep scorecards and predictive reasoning on existing paths.\n\n### Comparison contract\n- Define a comparison intermediate model in Python, not prompt text:\n  - resolved domain\n  - comparison axis: time, location, or metric subset\n  - entities/periods to compare\n  - canonical table title\n  - normalized rows/columns\n  - summary sentence(s)\n  - freshness/citation metadata\n- `comparison_agent` should decide what to compare and which domain tools to call, but Python should build the final table artifact.\n\n### Deterministic comparison builder\n- Add a shared comparison builder utility in the agents layer or orchestration layer.\n- Builder responsibilities:\n  - accept tool outputs from one domain only\n  - align records by comparison axis\n  - choose stable column headers\n  - map raw keys to user-facing metric labels\n  - emit canonical `artifact.type = \"table\"` shape\n  - generate a compact prose summary and freshness note\n- Builder should support these v1 patterns:\n  - time comparison within one location\n  - location comparison within one period\n  - month/year breakdown comparisons from summary endpoints\n- If the requested comparison cannot be formed from existing data, return a clean refusal/limitation in `response_text` with no malformed artifact.\n\n### Tool usage by domain\n- Weather:\n  - prefer `get_weather_summary` for month/year/season comparisons\n  - fall back to `get_weather_historical` only when summary data cannot answer the request\n- AQI:\n  - prefer `get_aqi_summary` for month/year comparisons\n  - use `get_aqi_historical` for short-window comparisons\n- Do not add new backend endpoints for v1 unless an uncovered same-domain comparison is impossible with current tools.\n- Do not support mixed-domain tables in v1.\n\n### Prompt updates\n- Update `chat_agent` prompt to hand comparison/table requests to `comparison_agent`.\n- Add a dedicated `comparison_agent` prompt that focuses on:\n  - extracting domain, entities, periods, and requested metrics\n  - calling only relevant domain tools\n  - returning structured comparison inputs, not free-form `summary/details` blobs\n- Remove reliance on legacy prompt patterns that emit raw dicts.\n\n### Backend compatibility\n- Keep the current backend parser compatibility layer for old malformed outputs during transition.\n- Treat the new `comparison_agent` output format as canonical and preferred.\n- Do not expand legacy-shape parsing further once the new comparison path is live unless a concrete regression is found.\n\n### Frontend behavior\n- Keep the existing `table` artifact renderer as the only rendering path for comparison tables.\n- Ensure refreshed/restored chats continue to render saved comparison responses correctly in live sessions.\n- If history replay later includes structured artifacts, reuse the same `table` artifact contract.\n\n## Interfaces / Public Types\n- Keep the public `/api/chat` response contract stable.\n- Canonical comparison response:\n  - `response_text: string`\n  - `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n  - `artifact.title: string`\n  - `artifact.columns: list[string]`\n  - `artifact.rows: list[list[string | number | null]]`\n  - optional `artifact.description`\n- Add internal comparison-agent structured output contract for orchestration only; it should not be exposed to the browser.\n\n## Test Plan\n- Agent/orchestration tests:\n  - comparison intent detection for `compare`, `vs`, `table`\n  - same-domain routing goes to `comparison_agent`\n  - non-comparison historical queries still route to existing domain agents\n- Comparison-builder tests:\n  - weather month-vs-month comparison in one location\n  - AQI location-vs-location comparison for one period\n  - missing month or sparse data handling\n  - unsupported mixed-domain request returns a limitation, not malformed output\n- Backend/API tests:\n  - `/api/chat` accepts canonical `table` artifacts from the new comparison flow\n  - legacy compatibility still works during transition\n- Frontend tests:\n  - `table` artifact renders with correct headers/rows\n  - restored active session still displays comparison tables after refresh\n\n## Assumptions\n- V1 comparison scope is same-domain only.\n- Weather and AQI are the first-class comparison domains in v1.\n- Existing summary/historical tools are sufficient for the first iteration.\n- Comparison table generation should be deterministic in code, with the LLM used for intent extraction and tool orchestration rather than free-form table authoring.\n"
    }
  }
}
```

comparison intent detection for `compare`, `vs`, `table`\n  - same-domain routing goes to `comparison_agent`\n  - non-comparison historical queries still route to existing domain agents\n- Comparison-builder tests:\n  - weather month-vs-month comparison in one location\n  - AQI location-vs-location comparison for one period\n  - missing month or sparse data handling\n  - unsupported mixed-domain request returns a limitation, not malformed output\n- Backend/API tests:\n  - `/api/chat` accepts canonical `table` artifacts from the new comparison flow\n  - legacy compatibility still works during transition\n- Frontend tests:\n  - `table` artifact renders with correct headers/rows\n  - restored active session still displays comparison tables after refresh\n\n## Assumptions\n- V1 comparison scope is same-domain only.\n- Weather and AQI are the first-class comparison domains in v1.\n- Existing summary/historical tools are sufficient for the first iteration.\n- Comparison table generation should be deterministic in code, with the LLM used for intent extraction and tool orchestration rather than free-form table authoring.\n</proposed_plan>"
```json
      }
    ],
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

```json
      {

        "text": "<proposed_plan>\n# Comparison Capability End-to-End\n\n## Summary\nBuild a first-class comparison flow inside the existing `metrosense_agent` stack for same-domain comparisons, with a dedicated internal `comparison_agent` backed by deterministic Python table-building utilities.\n\nV1 scope:\n- Support arbitrary comparisons within a single domain at a time\n- Primary initial domains: weather and AQI, because summary endpoints already exist and fit tabular comparisons well\n- Output must always be canonical MetroSense chat payloads: concise `response_text` plus `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n\nChosen defaults:\n- Architecture: `comparison_agent` for intent/tool orchestration + deterministic backend/agent-side comparison builder for table construction\n- No new public browser-facing API\n- No prompt-only comparison generation except as a fallback; table assembly should be code-driven\n\n## Key Changes\n### Agent orchestration\n- Add an internal `comparison_agent` under `chat_agent`.\n- Extend intent routing to explicitly detect comparison/table requests:\n  - `compare`, `comparison`, `vs`, `versus`, `table`, `month over month`, `year over year`, `location A vs location B`\n- Route only same-domain comparisons to `comparison_agent` in v1.\n- Keep scorecards and predictive reasoning on existing paths.\n\n### Comparison contract\n- Define a comparison intermediate model in Python, not prompt text:\n  - resolved domain\n  - comparison axis: time, location, or metric subset\n  - entities/periods to compare\n  - canonical table title\n  - normalized rows/columns\n  - summary sentence(s)\n  - freshness/citation metadata\n- `comparison_agent` should decide what to compare and which domain tools to call, but Python should build the final table artifact.\n\n### Deterministic comparison builder\n- Add a shared comparison builder utility in the agents layer or orchestration layer.\n- Builder responsibilities:\n  - accept tool outputs from one domain only\n  - align records by comparison axis\n  - choose stable column headers\n  - map raw keys to user-facing metric labels\n  - emit canonical `artifact.type = \"table\"` shape\n  - generate a compact prose summary and freshness note\n- Builder should support these v1 patterns:\n  - time comparison within one location\n  - location comparison within one period\n  - month/year breakdown comparisons from summary endpoints\n- If the requested comparison cannot be formed from existing data, return a clean refusal/limitation in `response_text` with no malformed artifact.\n\n### Tool usage by domain\n- Weather:\n  - prefer `get_weather_summary` for month/year/season comparisons\n  - fall back to `get_weather_historical` only when summary data cannot answer the request\n- AQI:\n  - prefer `get_aqi_summary` for month/year comparisons\n  - use `get_aqi_historical` for short-window comparisons\n- Do not add new backend endpoints for v1 unless an uncovered same-domain comparison is impossible with current tools.\n- Do not support mixed-domain tables in v1.\n\n### Prompt updates\n- Update `chat_agent` prompt to hand comparison/table requests to `comparison_agent`.\n- Add a dedicated `comparison_agent` prompt that focuses on:\n  - extracting domain, entities, periods, and requested metrics\n  - calling only relevant domain tools\n  - returning structured comparison inputs, not free-form `summary/details` blobs\n- Remove reliance on legacy prompt patterns that emit raw dicts.\n\n### Backend compatibility\n- Keep the current backend parser compatibility layer for old malformed outputs during transition.\n- Treat the new `comparison_agent` output format as canonical and preferred.\n- Do not expand legacy-shape parsing further once the new comparison path is live unless a concrete regression is found.\n\n### Frontend behavior\n- Keep the existing `table` artifact renderer as the only rendering path for comparison tables.\n- Ensure refreshed/restored chats continue to render saved comparison responses correctly in live sessions.\n- If history replay later includes structured artifacts, reuse the same `table` artifact contract.\n\n## Interfaces / Public Types\n- Keep the public `/api/chat` response contract stable.\n- Canonical comparison response:\n  - `response_text: string`\n  - `artifact.type = \"table\"`\n  - `artifact.title: string`\n  - `artifact.columns: list[string]`\n  - `artifact.rows: list[list[string | number | null]]`\n  - optional `artifact.description`\n- Add internal comparison-agent structured output contract for orchestration only; it should not be exposed to the browser.\n\n## Test Plan\n- Agent/orchestration tests:\n  - comparison intent detection for `compare`, `vs`, `table`\n  - same-domain routing goes to `comparison_agent`\n  - non-comparison historical queries still route to existing domain agents\n- Comparison-builder tests:\n  - weather month-vs-month comparison in one location\n  - AQI location-vs-location comparison for one period\n  - missing month or sparse data handling\n  - unsupported mixed-domain request returns a limitation, not malformed output\n- Backend/API tests:\n  - `/api/chat` accepts canonical `table` artifacts from the new comparison flow\n  - legacy compatibility still works during transition\n- Frontend tests:\n  - `table` artifact renders with correct headers/rows\n  - restored active session still displays comparison tables after refresh\n\n## Assumptions\n- V1 comparison scope is same-domain only.\n- Weather and AQI are the first-class comparison domains in v1.\n- Existing summary/historical tools are sufficient for the first iteration.\n- Comparison table generation should be deterministic in code, with the LLM used for intent extraction and tool orchestration rather than free-form table authoring.\n</proposed_plan>"
      }
    ],
    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}
```

## Entry 546

**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T18:49:38.509Z",
  "type": "turn_context",
  "payload": {
    "turn_id": "019cde3b-abcb-7122-a201-145f57745684",
    "cwd": "/home/dell/ai-track-metrosense",
    "current_date": "2026-03-12",
    "timezone": "Asia/Kolkata",
    "approval_policy": "on-request",
    "sandbox_policy": {
      "type": "workspace-write",
      "writable_roots": [
        "/home/dell/.codex/memories"
      ],
      "network_access": false,
      "exclude_tmpdir_env_var": false,
      "exclude_slash_tmp": false
    },
    "model": "gpt-5.4",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "plan",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.4",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is the working MetroSense codebase: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer (✅ **exists and wired**, current status: skeleton with google_search tool only)\n- Treat the current implementation as the baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n- **Status**: ~70% complete. Auth flow, backend-to-agent routing, database schema, internal data routes, custom domain agent tools, structured response contract, and full subagent hierarchy are functional. Data seeding (script ready, not yet run), streaming, and E2E tests remain.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - The agents service runs behind a lightweight proxy that enforces the `X-Internal-Token` shared secret. The backend calls the proxy on port 8020, and the proxy forwards to the ADK server on port 8021. `/health` stays public for simple availability checks.\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8021 .`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=your-internal-token-here && uv run uvicorn app.proxy:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n\n### Implemented (✅)\n- **Backend:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me with JWT + httponly cookies), health checks, database schema (10+ models for weather/AQI/flooding/outages/traffic, sessions, conversation history), `/api/chat` endpoint with full `RiskCardPayload` and `ArtifactPayload` response schema\n- **Backend Internal Routes:** All `/internal/*` data routes (weather, AQI, lakes, floods, outages, traffic, ward profile, locations) — token-gated, consumed only by agent tools\n- **Backend Services:** `data_service.py` (all domain DB queries + neighbourhood→zone mapping), `conversation_service.py` (session upsert + turn append to `ConversationHistory`), `agent_proxy.py` (Level-2 and Level-1 ADK response parsing)\n- **Backend-to-Agent:** Proxy service with `X-Internal-Token` validation, session routing to ADK\n- **Agent Subagent Hierarchy:** Five-agent system (`root_agent → chat_agent → flood_vulnerability_agent / heat_health_agent / infrastructure_agent / logistics_agent`) using `gemini-2.5-flash`\n- **Agent Tools:** `flood_tools` (lake hydrology, flood incidents), `heat_tools` (AQI current/historical, ward profile), `infra_tools` (power outage events), `logistics_tools` (traffic current/corridor), `shared/weather_tools` (weather current/historical), `shared/location_tools` (resolve/list locations), `shared/document_tools` (list indexes, fetch section)\n- **Orchestration:** `routing.py` (keyword-based intent classification: flood/heat/infra/logistics/greeting), `response_contract.py` (`build_level2_response()` structured output builder)\n- **Prompts:** Domain-specific system prompts for all five agents; `CHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION` mandates JSON-only structured output with data freshness caveats and off-domain refusal\n- **Data Loader Script:** `server/scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py` — reads 6 CSVs and inserts reference + domain tables; run with `uv run python scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py --replace` (not yet executed)\n- **Frontend:** Chat UI (MessageBubble, MessageList, InputBar, ThinkingIndicator, SuggestedPrompts), auth context (login/signup/logout), RiskCard and ArtifactRenderer components, health polling\n- **Tests:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, health checks, basic chat endpoint\n\n### Planned (📋)\n- **Data Seeding:** `load_metrosense_dataset.py` exists and is ready to run; CSVs not yet loaded into PostgreSQL — run once before agent tools can return real data\n- **Document Ingestion:** Reference documents (`Documents_Metrosense/`) are read directly from the filesystem by `document_tools`; no DB ingestion needed\n- **Streaming:** Responses are buffered POST/response; SSE or WebSocket streaming not yet implemented\n- **Message Context Loading:** `ConversationHistory` model and `conversation_service` exist; conversation context not yet injected into agent session on new requests\n- **Structured Risk Card Generation:** End-to-end schema is wired (`RiskCardPayload` in routes, `build_level2_response()` in agents); requires data seeding + agent logic to populate fields\n- **E2E Tests:** Playwright configured; no test suites written yet\n- **Chat Response Tests:** Coverage for agent output parsing, risk card generation, artifact rendering\n\n### Current Test Coverage\n- Backend: Health, auth (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, basic chat routing\n- Frontend: Component renders (no E2E tests written yet)\n- Agents: smoke_test.py validates proxy routing only (not domain logic)\n\n### Next Steps (Recommended Order)\n1. **Run data loader** — `cd server && uv run python scripts/load_metrosense_dataset.py
```
